author: @chaitanyabhave2092
text: How do I verify that the intercalation has taken place? Is there any chemical or physical verification method?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Chaitanya Bhave what are you intercalating with?

replies.author: @chaitanyabhave2092
replies.text: Chlorine and iodine are the ones I've tried so far...I'll try bromine if I get a bromide salt

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Chaitanya Bhave conductivity increases

replies.author: @chaitanyabhave2092
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith oh yeah. That seems easy to check. Btw I found a way to make graphene in solution using your mechanical method- and it doesn't seem to require a homogenizer.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Chaitanya Bhave that's very cool - want to share?

author: @jimbob2
text: Hiya Robert, I tried this and it separated into black sludge at the bottom, and a bright yellow bromine water. Which one of these am I supposed to keep? The video was a little unclear. Thanks!

replies.author: @jimbob2
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Hm, I seem to be getting 2000ohm resistance with this 'ink'. Will washing it or anything else improve the conductivity?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sure - but so will heat treatment - but - to be honest if you check out the literature a 2k resistance is a pretty good result

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I would probably try vacuum filtration and microwave exfoliation - though a preheated furnace would work - but, mate, this is meant to encourage YOU to experiment and so it is not a cookery site with recipes to follow and you get a cake at the end of it - you will just have to try a few things and see how it works out

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - cheers mate

author: @danielserbin6840
text: Hi Robert. I've been following you work for months now and it is time to get off my butt and do some discovering. I am looking at the lightscribe method of making supercapacitors. I'm struggling to get the whole graphite-oxide, graphene-oxide, graphene in suspension thing. Would making graphene this way be suitable for the lightscribe method, or should I use one of your earlier methods of making graphene-oxide? Thanks and good luck in you future endeavors.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya Mate - if you want to use the lightscribe method the first thing you need is graphene oxide in water. You will have to make it - so use the method in my large scale graphene oxide method video

author: @staticnetwork
text: I happen to have graphite on hand, but it's 75 micron (275 mesh), do you think it would work or should I go out and get the ultra fine stuff?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sounds good - i would try it with that

replies.author: @staticnetwork
replies.text: I tried it and the material looks like yours, from the brown liquid to the black.  I couldn't find deionized water (I live in Oregon) so I used distilled water, but I easily found the sodium bromide at a pool supply store (http://amzn.com/B000V8BUF0 )

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cool

replies.author: @staticnetwork
replies.text: I tried to make this and I'm not certain if the particle size of the graphite was too large or if the sonication wasn't good enough, but my batch seems to be black at the bottom and bromine liquid at the top.  I've order 5 micron graphite and I'll attempt this again.

replies.author: @jimbob2
replies.text: staticnetwork I'm getting the same results as you. Is there a major difference with 5 micron graphite?

author: @jonbaud1
text: I have 75 micron graphite. Would that work. If not do you know a reliable place to buy 5 micron graphite.

author: @ns4235
text:
According to this paper, graphene can be made using graphite, detergent, and a blender. Maybe it's interesting.

http://hypermarkgo.com/news/graphene-smoothie/
references this paper
http://www.nature.com/nmat/journal/vaop/ncurrent/pdf/nmat3944.pdf

author: @keeganmaloney3440
text: is 25 khz suitable for this application?

author: @minnican
text: Just a thought..... won't the Van der Waals forces pull the graphene sheets back together or does the bromine stay attached to the surface of the graphene and hold them apart...?

author: @minnican
text: Having watched many many of your videos, this and part 1, definately seems to be the simplest way to make graphene. I must give this a go.... Thanks very much for all the time and effort.

author: @sketybel1
text: Have you ever experimented with graphene as a replacement for platinum as a catalyst for an oxygen reduction reaction? The idea of course is to make a cheap PEM fuel cell at home. I read that using iodine instead of bromine will allow the graphene to conduct 33% more current than platinum. I am of course no expert but, I would love to hear your take on the subject.

author: @moog2k
text:
How about this and your graphene
http://www.iflscience.com/technology/how-drop-seawater-graphene-generates-electricity

author: @sasjadv
text:
How much of the bromine will be left if you wash the graphene?
If you make a water-filter out of your ink you probably don't want any halogens in there.
And btw. how do you wash a solution?

author: @jushkya
text: Hi!,seems to be much cleaner method indeed! I followed the article you had mentioned,according to it, to the wt ratio of graphite to Br used it atleast 10 times less that what is used in your method. i.e. if we use the same Graphite/ Br ratio we shld be adding 0.2g of graphite and not 2g of graphite.. Is there any reason to add 10 times more?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it just needs to be in excess

author: @joeglory425
text: I suspect the bromine might be released as the individual graphene sheets are separated during sonication.  It then might be possible to reuse the bromine over and over.  This might not be practical on a small scale but on an industrial scale it could save a lot of money.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting idea

author: @danhunt5016
text: You have so many projects on the go. I don't know how you have enough time in the day!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: only really soap and ink mate

author: @artytomparis
text:
Looks really interesting. If that's the sort of sonic bath that jewelers use to clean metals after annealing, they are not expensive and easy to get. Great work demystifying Graphene.

I would contribute but I won't support indieogo. When you do a Kickstarter campaign, I'll definitely get involved.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It is a jeweler's sonicator.

author: @keeganmaloney3440
text: best tutorial yet, very simple and seemingly effective. I will be trying this within the week. I just have to figure out how to diy a sonicator or find one real cheap. I'll try to remember to post the results.

replies.text: there are two you should watch - ink basics and make a conductive ink - they are in the conductive inks and electronics playlist they should help a lot - the main one is on my homepage as the banner video and there is a link in the video to the basics vid it pops up on the screen while you are watching
